notes.title: Short Technical Note
notes.content: React hooks are functions that let you use state and other React features without writing a class. The useState hook allows you to add state to functional components. The useEffect hook lets you perform side effects in function components.

notes.title: Medium Academic Paper Summary
notes.content: Machine learning has revolutionized the field of artificial intelligence by enabling computers to learn patterns from data without explicit programming. Deep learning, a subset of machine learning, uses neural networks with multiple layers to model and understand complex patterns in data. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been particularly successful in image recognition tasks, achieving human-level performance in many domains. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and their variants like LSTM and GRU have shown remarkable success in natural language processing tasks. The transformer architecture, introduced in the paper 'Attention is All You Need', has become the foundation for modern language models like GPT and BERT. These models have demonstrated unprecedented capabilities in text generation, translation, and understanding. The key innovation of transformers is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the importance of different parts of the input sequence when making predictions. This has led to significant improvements in performance across various NLP tasks.

notes.title: Long Research Document
notes.content: The field of quantum computing represents one of the most significant technological frontiers of the 21st century. Unlike classical computers that use bits to represent information as either 0 or 1, quantum computers use quantum bits or qubits that can exist in a superposition of both states simultaneously. This fundamental difference allows quantum computers to process vast amounts of information in parallel, potentially solving certain problems exponentially faster than classical computers. The concept of quantum superposition is just one of the quantum mechanical phenomena that quantum computers exploit. Another crucial principle is quantum entanglement, where qubits become correlated in such a way that the quantum state of each qubit cannot be described independently. This creates a powerful computational resource that has no classical analog. Quantum interference is the third key principle, allowing quantum algorithms to amplify correct answers and cancel out wrong ones. The development of quantum algorithms has been a major focus of research since the 1990s. Shor's algorithm, developed by Peter Shor in 1994, demonstrated that a quantum computer could factor large integers exponentially faster than the best known classical algorithms. This has profound implications for cryptography, as many current encryption methods rely on the difficulty of factoring large numbers. Grover's algorithm, developed by Lov Grover in 1996, provides a quadratic speedup for searching unsorted databases. While not as dramatic as Shor's exponential speedup, Grover's algorithm has broad applications in optimization and machine learning. The physical implementation of quantum computers faces numerous challenges. Quantum states are extremely fragile and can be easily disrupted by environmental noise, a phenomenon known as decoherence. Maintaining quantum coherence requires operating at extremely low temperatures, often near absolute zero, and in highly controlled environments. Various physical platforms have been explored for building quantum computers, including superconducting circuits, trapped ions, photonic systems, and topological qubits. Each approach has its own advantages and challenges in terms of coherence time, gate fidelity, and scalability.

notes.title: Code Documentation
notes.content: The SummaryGenerator component is responsible for handling the user interface for generating AI summaries of note content. It manages the loading state during API calls, displays appropriate error messages when summarization fails, and provides user feedback throughout the process. The component accepts props including noteContent (the text to summarize), existingSummary (any previously generated summary), and onSummaryGenerated (callback function when a new summary is created). The component uses React hooks for state management, including useState for loading and error states, and useCallback for memoizing the summarization function. Error handling includes network timeouts, service unavailability, and invalid input validation. The component integrates with the broader note editing interface and maintains consistency with the application's design system.

notes.title: Special Characters and Unicode
notes.content: This note contains special characters: áéíóú, ñ, ç, ü, ß, and emojis 🚀 📝 💡. It also includes mathematical symbols: ∑, ∫, ∆, π, and currency symbols: $, €, £, ¥. Testing unicode handling: 中文, العربية, русский, हिन्दी. Code snippets with symbols: const sum = (a, b) => a + b; SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > 18;

notes.title: Extremely Long Note for Chunking Test
notes.content: This is an extremely long note designed to test the chunking functionality of the summarization service.
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

This note should exceed the token limit and require chunking into multiple segments for processing. The summarization service should handle this gracefully by splitting the text, processing each chunk, and then consolidating the results into a coherent final summary.
Sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.
The chunking algorithm should maintain context across segments and produce a summary that captures the key points from the entire document, not just individual chunks.
